rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit: the measure is to 'Commence implementation of the Financial Strategy on Climate Change (EFCC)' and 'update it every 5 years' (policy_statement; verbatim_text). This is an implementation/finance strategy measure ('Implementation Measures Climate Finance N°3'), i.e., an enabling mechanism for financing climate action, not a direct mitigation outcome. Therefore the primary_category 'Mitigation' is mismatched and should be reclassified to a category capturing climate finance/enabling capacity.
policy_statement: Commence implementation of the Financial Strategy on Climate Change (EFCC) in 2020 and update it every 5 years, with the first revision in 2021 (IM3).
mechanism_description: It begins implementation of a financial strategy on climate change and updates it periodically to help steer resources and strengthen capacity to deliver climate actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance/finance strategy → ↑ capital mobilized & allocated to climate measures → implementation of low-GHG investments → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Government establishes and periodically updates a climate finance strategy that sets priorities, rules, and institutional arrangements for mobilizing, allocating, and tracking climate-related funding. (2) Mediating process: Improved financial planning and instruments (e.g., budgeting, incentives, de-risking, disclosure/MRV, pipeline development) increase the volume and effectiveness of investment into low-emission projects and programs. (3) Outcome: Accelerated deployment of mitigation actions reduces fossil energy use and other emission sources, decreasing GHG emissions relative to baseline.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is unlocking and directing finance that enables implementation of emissions-reducing measures, leading to lower economy-wide GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: the mechanism chiefly works by mobilizing/allocating finance and strengthening delivery capacity, which then enables implementation of concrete actions whose largest aggregate climate effect is typically emissions reduction.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while the strategy can also fund adaptation, the mechanism itself is finance-to-action enabling; in most contexts the biggest climate-relevant payoff from scaled finance is mitigation investments that measurably reduce GHG emissions.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a finance strategy is explicitly restricted to resilience/adaptation financing only, adaptation could dominate; if restricted to efficiency-only programs, resource efficiency could dominate. Those are treated as exceptions requiring mechanism re-definition or policy-level evidence.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any “climate finance strategy implementation” mechanism, by design, increases financing and delivery capacity for climate actions; the dominant expected climate effect at scale is enabling mitigation deployment and associated emissions reductions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: whether individual financed actions are mitigation vs adaptation-heavy, and which sub-sector benefits most, must be assigned at the policy level using evidence on the financed portfolio, instruments, and targets.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be Adaptation-primary if the finance strategy is explicitly limited to resilience/hazard-loss reduction spending; could be Resource Efficiency-primary if narrowly focused on efficiency retrofits without broader mitigation framing. In many real strategies it is mixed; classification here follows the dominant expected emissions-reduction impact absent an explicit restriction.
key_indicators: “Implementation of the EFCC will commence in 2020”; “Financial Strategy on Climate Change (EFCC)”; “updated every 5 years”; “first revision… in 2021”
instance_edge_case_notes: No detail on specific financial tools (e.g., taxes, subsidies, disclosure rules) is provided, so classification stays at the higher-level planning/strategy instrument rather than a pricing mechanism or incentive program.
